original_hypothesis.title: calcLpTokenSupply divides by zero when one reserve is zero (but not both), potentially blocking operations in depleted-reserve states
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: calcLpTokenSupply only short-circuits when both reserves are zero. If one reserve is zero and the other is non-zero, the function proceeds and computes dP using division by (scaledReserves[x] * N), which will be zero and revert. Any state-changing path that calls calcLpTokenSupply (directly or via calcRate/calcReserveAtRatioSwap) can be forced to revert when the pool becomes one-sided.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Depleted-reserve states can occur organically (aggressive trading) or be induced by an attacker in thin pools. If withdrawals or critical maintenance operations depend on calcLpTokenSupply, this becomes a lock-up/availability risk.

proof_of_signal.pattern: only checks (r0==0 && r1==0); then dP = dP*D/(scaledReserves[0]*N) and /(scaledReserves[1]*N) => div by zero if either reserve is 0

preconditions: A Well operation reaches a state where one reserve is zero (or rounds to zero after scaling) while the other is nonzero
That operation (or a subsequent one) calls calcLpTokenSupply via Stable2
No alternate path exists that bypasses calcLpTokenSupply for withdrawals/emergency exits

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: missing guard for single-sided zero reserve
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: division by zero revert prevents LP supply computation
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no early return/revert with explicit error when reserves[i]==0

analysis_questions: Can a Stable2 Well reach a one-sided reserve state through swaps (consider min reserve constraints, if any, in Well.sol)?
Which state-changing functions in Well.sol rely on calcLpTokenSupply even for withdrawals/burns?
Does scaling with low decimals make small reserves round to zero and trigger this unexpectedly?
validation_requirements: Simulation: drive reserves[0] to 0 (or 1 unit) and verify which Well operations revert
Review Well.sol withdrawal logic for dependency on Stable2.calcLpTokenSupply
Add invariant test: if reserves[i]==0 XOR reserves[j]==0, operations should have defined behavior

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker repeatedly swaps to drain token0 to (near) zero. After this, any subsequent swap/liquidity/oracle update that calls calcLpTokenSupply reverts due to division by zero, freezing the pool until external liquidity is added in the depleted token.
prerequisites: Ability to trade enough to push one reserve to zero (more feasible in small pools)

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Indirect; may lock users from executing certain operations and prevent price/oracle updates
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All pool users during the one-sided state
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Recoverable by adding the depleted token; may be impossible if all state-changing paths revert

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No tests for single-sided zero reserves in lpTokenSupply/rate paths

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Depends on pool depth; low for small pools
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Multi-transaction to deplete reserve then trigger reverting call
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Monitor reserves approaching zero and reverting transactions referencing Stable2

validation_result.impact_summary: calcLpTokenSupply reverts with division by zero when one reserve is zero and the other is non-zero, blocking all dependent operations (calcRate, calcReserveAtRatioSwap, calcReserveAtRatioLiquidity) and potentially trapping funds.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The hypothesis correctly identifies that calcLpTokenSupply only short-circuits when BOTH reserves are zero (`reserves[0] == 0 && reserves[1] == 0`). When only one reserve is zero, the function passes both guards (the both-zero check and the sumReserves==0 check) and enters the Newton iteration loop. Inside the loop, `dP = dP * lpTokenSupply / (scaledReserves[0] * N)` will divide by zero if scaledReserves[0] is 0 (and similarly for scaledReserves[1]). This causes an EVM revert. The developer left a comment acknowledging this: 'If division by 0, this will be borked: only withdrawal will work. And that is good.' However, this comment is misleading because calcLpTokenSupply is called by calcRate, which is called by calcReserveAtRatioLiquidity and calcReserveAtRatioSwap. Any Well operation that depends on these math functions will also revert, potentially including operations needed for recovery. The code is defective because it lacks a proper guard or graceful handling for the single-sided zero reserve case.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal is accurate. Lines ~74-83 of Stable2.sol show the exact pattern described. Line 74: `if (reserves[0] == 0 && reserves[1] == 0) return 0;` only catches the both-zero case. Line 82: `if (sumReserves == 0) return 0;` catches scaled-to-zero case but not single-sided zero. Lines 85-86: `dP = dP * lpTokenSupply / (scaledReserves[0] * N);` and `dP = dP * lpTokenSupply / (scaledReserves[1] * N);` will divide by zero when either scaledReserves[0] or scaledReserves[1] is zero.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: When reserves[0] > 0 and reserves[1] == 0 (or vice versa), the function passes the initial both-zero check and the sumReserves check, then attempts to divide by (scaledReserves[x] * N) where scaledReserves[x] is 0, causing an EVM revert. The comment on line 85 acknowledges this but the behavior is still a code defect.

calling_sequence: 1. calcLpTokenSupply called with reserves = [1000e18, 0] and data = abi.encode(18, 18)
3. Line 75-76: decodeWellData returns decimals = [18, 18], getScaledReserves returns [1000e18, 0]
7. Line 86: dP = 1000e18 * 1000e18 / (0 * 2) → DIVISION BY ZERO → REVERT
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: Any call path reaching calcLpTokenSupply with one zero reserve triggers the division by zero. This includes: (1) direct calls to calcLpTokenSupply, (2) calcRate at Stable2.sol:145 which calls calcLpTokenSupply at line 148, (3) calcReserveAtRatioSwap at Stable2.sol:159 which calls calcLpTokenSupply at line 173, (4) calcReserveAtRatioLiquidity at Stable2.sol:213 which calls calcRate at line 240 which calls calcLpTokenSupply.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided for Stable2-based wells specifically, but simulation scripts show Stable2 deployed with abi.encode(18, 18) data. The defect is inherent in the code logic regardless of configuration.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack scenario is realistic: an attacker (or organic market activity) drains one token reserve to zero through repeated swaps. In a thin/small pool, this is feasible with modest capital. Once one reserve reaches zero, any subsequent call to calcLpTokenSupply, calcRate, calcReserveAtRatioSwap, or calcReserveAtRatioLiquidity reverts. This blocks oracle updates (pumps relying on calcRate), swap operations, and potentially withdrawal operations that depend on LP supply calculation. The pool remains frozen until someone adds liquidity for the depleted token, but if the add-liquidity path itself requires calcLpTokenSupply, recovery may also be blocked.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH - When one reserve is zero, all mathematical functions in Stable2 that depend on calcLpTokenSupply revert. This includes rate calculations used by oracles/pumps, reserve-at-ratio calculations used by external protocols (Beanstalk), and potentially swap/withdrawal operations in Well.sol. The developer's comment acknowledges this but claims 'only withdrawal will work,' which may be incorrect if withdrawal logic requires LP supply calculation. This creates a denial-of-service condition for the entire pool.
recommended_mitigations: Add an explicit check at the start of calcLpTokenSupply: `if (reserves[0] == 0 || reserves[1] == 0) return 0;` or `if (scaledReserves[0] == 0 || scaledReserves[1] == 0) return 0;` to handle single-sided zero reserves gracefully
Alternatively, revert with a descriptive error message: `require(scaledReserves[0] > 0 && scaledReserves[1] > 0, 'Stable2: zero reserve');` so callers can handle the error appropriately
Verify that Well.sol withdrawal paths can function when calcLpTokenSupply reverts, and if not, implement emergency withdrawal logic that bypasses the well function
Consider implementing minimum reserve constraints in Well.sol to prevent either reserve from reaching zero through normal swap operations
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy Stable2LUT1 and Stable2 contracts. 2. Call calcLpTokenSupply([1000e18, 0], abi.encode(18, 18)) and verify it reverts with division by zero. 3. Call calcLpTokenSupply([0, 1000e18], abi.encode(18, 18)) and verify it also reverts. 4. Call calcRate([1000e18, 0], 0, 1, abi.encode(18, 18)) and verify it reverts (propagating from calcLpTokenSupply). 5. Deploy a full Well with Stable2 and attempt to deplete one reserve via swaps, then verify all pool operations revert.
uncertainties: Well.sol is not in scope - we cannot confirm exactly which Well operations (addLiquidity, removeLiquidity, swap) call calcLpTokenSupply and would be blocked
The developer's comment 'only withdrawal will work' suggests there may be a withdrawal path in Well.sol that bypasses calcLpTokenSupply, but we cannot verify this without the Well source code
Whether reserves can actually reach zero through normal Well swap operations depends on minimum reserve constraints (if any) implemented in Well.sol
additional_testing_needed: Review Well.sol source code to determine which operations depend on calcLpTokenSupply and whether emergency withdrawal paths exist
Test whether Well.sol swap functions allow reserves to reach zero or if there are implicit guards (e.g., requiring output < reserve)
Test with tokens of varying decimal precision (e.g., 6 decimals) to check if scaling can cause reserves to round to zero even with small positive balances
Fuzz test calcLpTokenSupply with edge case reserves near zero to characterize the failure boundary
